title: Slab relief nameplate
subtitle: A lettered clay plaque with raised text for a door, gate, or shelf
excerpt: A flat air-dry clay slab plaque with raised letter relief, suitable as a house name sign, a shelf label, or a decorative plaque. The letters are built up from thin clay strips or cut from a second slab and attached to the base. No kiln required. Finished plaque approximately 200 mm long by 80 mm tall.
sourceNotes: Relief lettering technique derived from first principles. Standard decorative ceramic plaque practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness and cut to shape.
Scratching the joining surfaces with a needle tool and brushing them with clay slurry before pressing together.
Fully air-dried and no longer damp. For air-dry clay, this is the structural finish state.

content.text: A relief nameplate has the text standing proud of a flat

base. Each letter is cut from a second thin slab and fixed to the base with score and slip. Air-dry clay is not waterproof, so this plaque is for indoor use or for a covered outdoor space only. If the plaque will be used near moisture, apply two coats of clear exterior acrylic varnish after finishing.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay for better letter-edge strength

content.text: Roll the main clay between the 8 mm guides to form the base slab. Cut a rectangle 200 mm long by 80 mm tall. For an arched top, cut a gentle curve across the top edge with the fettling knife. Leave the base slab flat on the board while preparing the letters.

Print the text in a clear block typeface at the desired size, approximately 40 mm tall. Cut out each letter template from the paper. Roll a second layer of clay between the 4 mm guides. Lay each paper template on the thin slab and cut around it with the fettling knife, pressing straight down through the template and clay. Peel away the paper and set each letter on the base slab to check spacing before fixing them.

to the back of each letter and to the corresponding area of the base slab. Press each letter firmly into position. Run a fingertip around the edge where the letter meets the base to blend the joint and prevent the letters from lifting as the clay dries.

While the clay is still soft, pierce two hanging holes near the top corners of the base slab with the needle tool, about 10 mm from each corner. These allow the plaque to be hung from screws or cord. Make the holes at least 5 mm in diameter so they do not close during drying.

Dry the plaque flat on the board. The base slab and letter layer will shrink at slightly different rates; dry slowly to reduce differential cracking. Cover with a loose plastic sheet for the first 12 hours, then uncover and allow to reach

Sand any rough edges carefully with fine sandpaper, including the sides of each letter. Paint the plaque with acrylic paint; a dark base colour with the letter surfaces painted a contrasting tone emphasises the relief. Apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish. For outdoor use, choose an exterior-grade varnish.
Press a simple border stamp around the perimeter of the base slab before attaching the letters.
Cut a house number only, without a name, for a larger numeral display.
Use paper clay for both layers to reduce cracking at letter joins.

rows.problem: Letters lift away from the base during drying
rows.cause: Score and slip was insufficient, or the letter clay was much wetter than the base clay
rows.fix: Blend the letter-to-base junction with a fingertip on both sides of each letter immediately after fixing. Match moisture levels before joining.

rows.cause: One surface dried faster than the other
rows.fix: Dry on a flat board and place a sheet of foam or cloth under the slab. Cover loosely for a slower even dry.

rows.problem: Letter edges crack at sharp corners
rows.cause: Sharp corners in the letter shapes are stress points
rows.fix: Round the corners of each cut letter slightly with a damp fingertip after cutting.
